passages.explanation: Jesus uses a pastoral metaphor. Someone who doesn't enter the sheep pen through the gate but climbs in another way is a thief. The real shepherd enters through the gate, the gatekeeper opens for him, and the sheep recognise his voice. He calls them by name and leads them out, walking ahead of them. They won't follow a stranger because they don't recognise his voice. John notes that Jesus' audience didn't understand what he was saying.

Jesus explains the metaphor by identifying himself as the gate. Anyone who enters through him will be saved and will find pasture. Everyone who came before making the same claims were thieves and robbers, but the sheep didn't listen to them. The thief comes only to steal, kill, and destroy. Jesus came so that people can have life — and have it abundantly. The contrast is between those who exploit the flock and the one who provides for it.

Jesus shifts the metaphor: now he's not just the gate but the good shepherd. The good shepherd dies for the sheep. A hired worker runs when the wolf comes because the sheep aren't his — he doesn't actually care about them. Jesus knows his sheep and they know him, the same way he and the Father know each other. He says he has other sheep not in this fold — likely referring to non-Jewish believers — and they will also hear his voice, forming one flock under one shepherd. He states clearly that no one takes his life from him. He lays it down voluntarily and has the authority to take it up again. This is a command he received from the Father.

The crowd divides again. Many say he's demon-possessed and insane. Others point out that a demon couldn't open the eyes of a blind man — referencing the healing in chapter 9. The division is becoming a pattern: the same evidence leads different people to opposite conclusions.

It's now winter, during the Feast of Dedication (Hanukkah). The Jewish authorities surround Jesus in Solomon's porch and demand a direct answer: are you the Christ or not? Jesus says he already told them and his works testify to it, but they don't believe because they aren't his sheep. His sheep hear his voice, he knows them, they follow him, and he gives them eternal life. They will never perish and no one can snatch them from his hand — or from the Father's hand. Then he makes the statement that triggers the confrontation: 'I and the Father are one.'

They pick up stones to kill him. Jesus asks which of his good works they're stoning him for. They say it's not for any work — it's for blasphemy, because he's a man claiming to be God. Jesus responds with an argument from their own Scripture: Psalm 82 calls human judges 'gods,' and Scripture can't be broken. If Scripture can use that word for ordinary people, why is it blasphemy when someone the Father set apart and sent into the world says 'I am the Son of God'? He offers a practical test: if his works don't match the Father's, don't believe him. But if they do, believe the works even if they can't accept his words — so they can understand that the Father is in him and he is in the Father.

They try to seize him again but he escapes. Jesus withdraws across the Jordan to the area where John the Baptist first baptised. Many people come to him there. They observe that John the Baptist never performed miraculous signs, but everything John said about Jesus has turned out to be true. Many believe in him in that place.
